Farn Polrrrcer- PRACTTcEs CouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
July 11,2007
Wesley David Wardall
17069 Lambert Road
Ione, Califomia95640
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-07-096
Dear Mr. Wardall:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the revolving door
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the'Act").I As your req.r".1 seeks general
assistance and does not provide specific information about partiiular pro-."Jding. o,
appearances, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2 This ietter is
based solely on the facts presented to us in your request. The Commission does not act as
a finder of fact when issuing advice or informal assistance. (In re Oglesby (1g75) |
FPPC Ops. 71.)
Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws
such as common law conflicts of interest, Government Code Section 1090, or public
Contract Code Section 10411.
QUESTION
what restrictions would the Act's "permanen t, and.,one-year,, revolving door
bans place upon you as a former Deputy Chief of Engineering and Manufacturing for the
california Department of Forestry and Fire protection ("cal Fire,,)?
CONCLUSION
The "permanent" and "one-year" bans appry to you. However, you may work as
a consultant for a private client on projects involving Cal Fire as long ur yo, do not
' Governm"ot Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14, Reg. 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
@@


File No. I-07-096
Page No. 2
appear before or communicate with Cal Fire or its employees, and the proceeding is not
one that would be subject to the restrictions of the "permanentban," ur dirr.rrr.d below.
FACTS
Cuffent-Employment, Experience and Certifications: Your title is Deputy
Chief, Engineering andManufacturing for Cal Fire. Prior to joining Cal Fire, you
worked for Lockheed Aircraft as a practicing aerospace engineer. io., are a Flderal
Aviation Administration ("FAA') certificated aircz:aft. structures engineer (FAA DER).
You are also an FAA licensed flight engineer and FAA licensed airline transport piloi.
You are the only Cal Fire person with the knowledge and expertise to inspect
airctaft and to make an airworthiness determination. Ifmajor 
"orrosiorr, cracks or other
damage is found, you are the only Cal Fire ernployee that candesign structural repairs
and retum the ar,Lrcraft. to service. You also have expertise in new part manufacturing,
quality control, source control, material verification, and inspe ction/ acceptance of new
parts.
In a telephone conversation on July 2,2007, you indicated that you are a
designated employee of Cal Fire required to file annual Statements of Economic
Interests.
Caruent Duties: As Deputy Chief, Engineering and Manufacturing for Cal Fire,
you oversee and manage all aerospace engineering and manufacturing opeiations. you
also insure continuous airworthiness of a fleet of 53 firefighting aircraft, make aircraft
assessments, design repairs and modifications, select and prescribe materials and material
processes for parts manufacturing, inspect new manufactured parts for conformity to
engineered drawings and process specifications. You also actis FAA repair station
manager for operations of the state's FAA certified aircraft repair station. you also test
fly and operate various firefighting aircraft.
You also indicate that you'have no fiscal budget," that you do not bid any work
(since that is done by people in your agency in charge of procurement), and that you do
not review or approve invoices for Cal Fire's prime contractor. In addition, you write
that you'have no business, fiscal or contract administrative responsibilities or
involvement." You also write that you did not develop or partiiipate in the last major
contract for Cal Fire's prime aircraft service contractor, and that you have not
participated in the current contract re-bid process.
Subsequent Employment: Youhave an opportunity to work as a consulting
engineer for DynCorp Aviation Services ("DynCorp"), the current state contractor
providing aircraft. and pilot services to Cal Fire. Your work for DynCorp would be
limited to fl ying air cr aft , inspecting air cr aft , making air cr aft condilion aisessments,
designing and approving repairs and modifications to aircraft, and inspecting aircraft
parts manufacturing operations.


File No. I-07-096
Page No. 3
You state that if you went to work for DynCorp you would exclude yourself from
the following actions:
. 
any business contacts with any Cal Fire officer or employee;
. 
afiy invoicing for services or materials;
. 
any attempt to meet with, communicate with, or influence any contract, or
business decision;
. 
arty involvement in the repair costs, schedules or parts used in aircraft.
You have not provided any details regarding any specific project: (1) you worked
on (or are currently working on) at Cal Fire, or (2) that you might work on while
employed at DynCorp if you accept a position there.
ANALYSIS
Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-
governmentalrestrictions under the Act,r colloquially known as the "revolving door',
prohibitions.a we begin our analysis with a description of the permanent ban.-
I. The Permanent Ban on 66Switching SidesD
The first post-employment restriction under the Act is a permanent prohibition on
influencing any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which the administrative
official participated while in state service. (Sections 87401 and87402;Reg. 18741.1.) In
other words, a public official may never "switch sides" in a proceeding after leaving siate
service. Sections 87401 and 87402 respectively provide that:
"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his
or her employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as agent
or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person (other than the
State of california) before any court or state administrative agency or
any officer or employee thereof by making any formal or informal
appearance, or by making any oral or written communication with the
intent to influence, in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding if both of the following apply:
"(a) The State of california is a party or has a direct and substantial
interest.
3 In addition, section 87407 prohrbits public officials from making, participating in making, or
using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating
employment, or have any arrangement concerning employment. (Section 87407;Reg. 187i7.)
a A general discussion of the elements of the two restrictions is set forth in the attached',Leaving
Your State Job? Post-Employment Reskictions May Affect You." We have attached this document for
your information.


File No. I-07-096
Page No. 4
"(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state administrative
offi cial participated." (Sectio n 87 401 .)
"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his
or her emplo5rment or term of office shall for compensation aid, advise,
counsel, consult or assist in representing any other person (except the
State of Califomia) in anyproceeding in which the official would be
prohibited from appearing under Section g74oL " (Section g7402.)
section 87400, subdivision (c) defines 'Judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding" as follows:
"(c) 'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding, means any
proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination,
contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or
other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court
or state administrative agency, including but not limited to, any
proceeding governed by chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of
Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code.,,
An official is considered to have "particrpated" in a proceeding if the official took
part "personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of
confidential information as an officer or employee. . . ." (section g7400(d).) A former
state official who held a management position in a state administrative agency is deerned
to have participated in a proceeding if: (1) the proceeding was pending Uifore the agency
during his or her tenure; and (2) the proceeding was under his or her supervisory
authority. (Section 87a00@); Reg. 18741.1(a)(a).) A proceeding is de-med to-be under a
sup ervisor' s "supervi sory authority'' if the supervisor:
(A) Has duties that include primary responsibility within the agency
for directing the operation or function of the program where the
proceeding is initiated or conducted; or
(B) Has direct supervision of the person performing the
investigation, review, or other action involved in the proceeding
including, but not limited to, assigning the matter for which the required
conduct is taken; or
(C) Reviews, discusses, or authorizes any action in the proceeding;
or
(D) Has any contact with any of the participants in the proceeding
regarding the subject of the proceeding.
"supervisory authority'' does not include a supervisor, at ahigher
level within the agency's chain-of-command than the supervisor
identified in subsection (a)(a)(A) above, with responsibility for the
general oversight of the administrative actions or functions of a program


FileNo. I-07-096
Page No. 5
where the responsibilities concerning the specific or final review of the
proceeding are expressly delegated to other persons in the agency,s
structure (i.e. supervisors under subsection (a)(+)(A) above) unless the
higher level supervising official has actual involvement in the
proceeding as set forth in subsections (a)(a)(C) or (D) of this
regulation." (Reg. 187 41.1(a)(4)(A) - (D).)
"The permanent ban does not apply to a onew' proceeding even in cases where the
new proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had
participated. A onew' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban tl,pically involves
different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual issues from those
considered in previous proceedings." (Rzs/ Advice Letter,No. A-04-1g7; see also
Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.)
You indicate that your general responsibilities while with the state include and
have included: insuring continuous airworthiness of a fleet of 53 firefighting airuaft,
making aircraft assessments, designing repairs and modifications, selecting and
prescribing materials and material processes for parts manufacfuring, inspecting new
manufactured parts for conformity to engineered drawings and process specifications.
You also state that you act as FAA repair station manager for operations of the state's
FAA certified aircraft repair station. You also test fly and operate various firefighting
aircraft. From these facts, we cannot determine if the permanent ban applies to your
situation, as we are unable to identifu any specific proceeding in which you participated
while employed by the state.
If you believe that you have participated in any process or determination affecting
another party or parties that would qualiff as a proceeding under the Act, please seek
further advice.
II. The One-Year Ban or "Revolving Door,
A. Application of the One-Year Ban.
Section 87406 of the Act prohibits specified officials from acting as an agent or
attomey or otherwise representing, for compensation, "any other person, by making any
formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, biforl
any state administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof," for one-year after the
official left the agency's employrnent "if the appearance or cofirmunication is made for
the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action
or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit,
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property.,, An appearunce
before a state administrative agency does not include an appearance in a court of law or
before an administrative law judge. (Section 87a06@); see also Reg. 1g746.1).
Regulation 1 87 46.2(a) further provides :


File No. I-07-096
Page No. 6
"(a) Forpurposes of Government Code Section 87406, a formal or
informal appearance or oral or written communication is for the purpose
of influencing if it is made for the principal purpose of supporting,
promoting, influencing, modifuing, opposing, delayng, or advancing the
action or proceeding. An appearance or cornmunication includes, but is
not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, coresponding with
in writing or by electronic transmission, affending a meeting, and
delivering or sending any cornmunication." (Reg. ft7a6.2(a).)
You have indicated that you are a "designated employee" under the agency,s
conflict of interest code. As such, the Act's one-year ban applies to you. (Section
87 406(d)(1) ; Ree. t 87 a6.t (a)Q).)
Regulation 18746.1(b) provides guidance about when the prohibitions of the one-
yearban will apply. Under that regulation, an official covered by the one-year ban is
prohibited from makin g an appearance or communication if all of the following apply:
"(1) The official has left his or her state office or employment, which
means he or she has either permanently left state service or is on aleave
of absence.[5]
"(2)The appearance or communication is made within 12 months
after leaving state office or employment.
"(3) The public official is compensated, or promised compensation,
for the appeararrce or cofllmunication. However, a payment made for
necessary travel, meals, and accommodations received directly in
connection with voluntary services is not prohibited or limited by this
section.
"(4) The appearance or communication is made on behalf of any
person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person. An
appearance or communication made by a public official solely to
represent his or her personal interests, as defined in2 cal. code Regs.
section 18702.4, subdivision (bxl), is not prohibited or limited by this
section.
"(5) The appearance or communication is made for the purpose of
influencing, as defined in2 Cal. Code Regs. Section 18746.2, any
legislative or administrative action, or any discretionary act involving
5 The one-year period commences when an employee is no longer authorized to perform the
duties of his or her state job, even if the person continues to receive compensation for accrued leave credits.
(Reg. 1 8746. 1 (bX 1 ); Coler Advice Letter, I-07-089.)


File No. I-07-096
Page No. 7
the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grarfi or corrtract, or the sale or purchase ofgoods or property.
"(A) Services performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the
requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract, or sale
agreement may be excluded from the prohibitions of this regulation,
provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings.
However, the prohibitions of Government code Sections g7401 and
87402 [the permanent ban on "switching sides,,] may apply.
"(6) The appearance or communication is made before any officer or
employee of any of the following:
"(A) Any state administrative agency that the public official worked
for or represented during the 12 months before leaving state office or
employrnent. . ..
"(B) Any state adminiskative agency which budget, personnel, and
other operations are subject to the direction and control of u.ry agency
described in subdivision (b)(6)(A). . . .
"(C) Any state administrative agency subject to the direction and
control of the Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the
Governor's office during the 72 months before leaving state office or
employment." (Reg. lS7 46.1 (b).)
B. Agencies Covered by the One-year Ban.
A designated employee's state administrative agency means the agency for which
he or she worked, or any board or commission under the agency,s control. (dould
Advice Letter, No. A-96-077.) The agency you work for is cai Fire. As a rlsult, you
may not for one year, for compensation, act as representative or agent for any person
before Cal Fire "for the purpose of influencing administrative or ligislatir" u"iorr, o.
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, um"rd*"rrt, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, glrffit, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property." (Section 87406.) You may, however, appear before state adminiitrative
agencies other than Cal Fire, provided there is no prohibited conduct before an officer or
employee of Cal Fire involved in that appearance.
C. Communications Covered by the One-year Ban.
Communications restricted by the one-year ban include any formal or informal
appearance or oral or written communication made to influence legislative or
administrative action or any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment,


File No. I-07-096
Page No. 9
In summary, the one-year ban does not prevent you from requesting information
generally available to the public about agency business from Cal Fire or otler state
agencies. Nor does it prohibit you from attending informational meetings of Cal Fire or
another state agency regarding existing laws, regulations, or policies, so-long as, when at
the meeting, you do not attempt to influence the agency's legislative or admlnistrative
action. Social conversations with employees of Cal Fire and other state agencies, not
intended to influence administrative or legislative action, also are not prohibited by the
ban. (Tobias Advice Letter, No. 4-96-089; see section g7406(dx1).)
If you have arty other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at
(916) 322-s660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Andreas C. Rockas
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
ACRjgl
Enclosures


File No. I-07-096
Page No. 8
awarding or revocation or a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of
goods or property. (Section 87406(d)(I).) These communications include, but are not
limited to, conversing directly or by telephone, corresponding by writing or e-mail,
attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communication. (Reg. 187a 6.2(a).)
A communication is considered to be for the putpose of influencing legislative or
administrative action "if it is made for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting,
influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action orproceeding.,,-'
(Reg. 18746.2(a).)
Not all communications to a former state administrative agency employer are
prohibited by the one-year ban. An appearance or communication before a former state
administrative agency employer, made as part of o'services performed to administer,
implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, g;arrt, contract, or
sale agteement may be excluded from the [one-year] prohibitions . . . provided the
services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or."rorution of any of these
actions or proceedings." (Reg. 18746.1(b)(5XA); williams Advice Letter,No. I-06-05g;
Quiring Advice Letter, No. 4-03-272; Hanan Advice Letter, No. I-00-209.) This is a
fact-specific query. To emphasize,the described activities still would be piohibited
under the permanent ban if while in state service, you participated in the proceedings.
(Thomas Advice Letter, No. 4-05-089.)
Certain other types of communications are not restricted under the one-year ban.
It is not considered a prohibited communication under the one-year ban, if an individual:
"(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or
similar public event for educational purposes or to disseminate research
and the subject matter does not pertain to a specific action or
proceeding;
"(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar
event;
"(3) Requests information conceming any matter of public record; or
"(4) communicates with the press." (Regulation 19746.2(b)(1)-(4).)
Finally, the Commission has advised that aformer agency official may draft
proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to the agency so long as the former
ernployee is not identified in connection with the client's efforts to influence
administrative action. (CookAdvice Letter, No. 4-95-327; Haruison Adviceletter, No.
A-92-289.) Similarly, the ex-employee may use his or her expertise to advise clients on
the procedural requirements, plans, or policies of the employee's former agency so long
as the employee is not identified with the employer's efforts to influence the agency.
(P"rry Advice Letter, No. 4-94-004.)

sections.analysis:
Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-
governmentalrestrictions under the Act,r colloquially known as the "revolving door',
prohibitions.a we begin our analysis with a description of the permanent ban.-
I. The Permanent Ban on 66Switching SidesD
The first post-employment restriction under the Act is a permanent prohibition on
influencing any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which the administrative
official participated while in state service. (Sections 87401 and87402;Reg. 18741.1.) In
other words, a public official may never "switch sides" in a proceeding after leaving siate
service. Sections 87401 and 87402 respectively provide that:
"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his
or her employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as agent
or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person (other than the
State of california) before any court or state administrative agency or
any officer or employee thereof by making any formal or informal
appearance, or by making any oral or written communication with the
intent to influence, in connection with any judicial, quasi-judicial or
other proceeding if both of the following apply:
"(a) The State of california is a party or has a direct and substantial
interest.
3 In addition, section 87407 prohrbits public officials from making, participating in making, or
using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons with whom they are negotiating
employment, or have any arrangement concerning employment. (Section 87407;Reg. 187i7.)
a A general discussion of the elements of the two restrictions is set forth in the attached',Leaving
Your State Job? Post-Employment Reskictions May Affect You." We have attached this document for
your information.
"(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state administrative
offi cial participated." (Sectio n 87 401 .)
"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his
or her emplo5rment or term of office shall for compensation aid, advise,
counsel, consult or assist in representing any other person (except the
State of Califomia) in anyproceeding in which the official would be
prohibited from appearing under Section g74oL " (Section g7402.)
section 87400, subdivision (c) defines 'Judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding" as follows:
"(c) 'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding, means any
proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination,
contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or
other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court
or state administrative agency, including but not limited to, any
proceeding governed by chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of
Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code.,,
An official is considered to have "particrpated" in a proceeding if the official took
part "personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of
confidential information as an officer or employee. . . ." (section g7400(d).) A former
state official who held a management position in a state administrative agency is deerned
to have participated in a proceeding if: (1) the proceeding was pending Uifore the agency
during his or her tenure; and (2) the proceeding was under his or her supervisory
authority. (Section 87a00@); Reg. 18741.1(a)(a).) A proceeding is de-med to-be under a
sup ervisor' s "supervi sory authority'' if the supervisor:
(A) Has duties that include primary responsibility within the agency
for directing the operation or function of the program where the
proceeding is initiated or conducted; or
(B) Has direct supervision of the person performing the
investigation, review, or other action involved in the proceeding
including, but not limited to, assigning the matter for which the required
conduct is taken; or
(C) Reviews, discusses, or authorizes any action in the proceeding;
or
(D) Has any contact with any of the participants in the proceeding
regarding the subject of the proceeding.
"supervisory authority'' does not include a supervisor, at ahigher
level within the agency's chain-of-command than the supervisor
identified in subsection (a)(a)(A) above, with responsibility for the
general oversight of the administrative actions or functions of a program


FileNo. I-07-096
where the responsibilities concerning the specific or final review of the
proceeding are expressly delegated to other persons in the agency,s
structure (i.e. supervisors under subsection (a)(+)(A) above) unless the
higher level supervising official has actual involvement in the
proceeding as set forth in subsections (a)(a)(C) or (D) of this
regulation." (Reg. 187 41.1(a)(4)(A) - (D).)
"The permanent ban does not apply to a onew' proceeding even in cases where the
new proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had
participated. A onew' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban tl,pically involves
different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual issues from those
considered in previous proceedings." (Rzs/ Advice Letter,No. A-04-1g7; see also
Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.)
You indicate that your general responsibilities while with the state include and
have included: insuring continuous airworthiness of a fleet of 53 firefighting airuaft,
making aircraft assessments, designing repairs and modifications, selecting and
prescribing materials and material processes for parts manufacfuring, inspecting new
manufactured parts for conformity to engineered drawings and process specifications.
You also state that you act as FAA repair station manager for operations of the state's
FAA certified aircraft repair station. You also test fly and operate various firefighting
aircraft. From these facts, we cannot determine if the permanent ban applies to your
situation, as we are unable to identifu any specific proceeding in which you participated
while employed by the state.
If you believe that you have participated in any process or determination affecting
another party or parties that would qualiff as a proceeding under the Act, please seek
further advice.
II. The One-Year Ban or "Revolving Door,
A. Application of the One-Year Ban.
Section 87406 of the Act prohibits specified officials from acting as an agent or
attomey or otherwise representing, for compensation, "any other person, by making any
formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, biforl
any state administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof," for one-year after the
official left the agency's employrnent "if the appearance or cofirmunication is made for
the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action
or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit,
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property.,, An appearunce
before a state administrative agency does not include an appearance in a court of law or
before an administrative law judge. (Section 87a06@); see also Reg. 1g746.1).
Regulation 1 87 46.2(a) further provides :
"(a) Forpurposes of Government Code Section 87406, a formal or
informal appearance or oral or written communication is for the purpose
of influencing if it is made for the principal purpose of supporting,
promoting, influencing, modifuing, opposing, delayng, or advancing the
action or proceeding. An appearance or cornmunication includes, but is
not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, coresponding with
in writing or by electronic transmission, affending a meeting, and
delivering or sending any cornmunication." (Reg. ft7a6.2(a).)
You have indicated that you are a "designated employee" under the agency,s
conflict of interest code. As such, the Act's one-year ban applies to you. (Section
87 406(d)(1) ; Ree. t 87 a6.t (a)Q).)
Regulation 18746.1(b) provides guidance about when the prohibitions of the one-
yearban will apply. Under that regulation, an official covered by the one-year ban is
prohibited from makin g an appearance or communication if all of the following apply:
"(1) The official has left his or her state office or employment, which
means he or she has either permanently left state service or is on aleave
of absence.[5]
"(2)The appearance or communication is made within 12 months
after leaving state office or employment.
"(3) The public official is compensated, or promised compensation,
for the appeararrce or cofllmunication. However, a payment made for
necessary travel, meals, and accommodations received directly in
connection with voluntary services is not prohibited or limited by this
section.
"(4) The appearance or communication is made on behalf of any
person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person. An
appearance or communication made by a public official solely to
represent his or her personal interests, as defined in2 cal. code Regs.
section 18702.4, subdivision (bxl), is not prohibited or limited by this
section.
"(5) The appearance or communication is made for the purpose of
influencing, as defined in2 Cal. Code Regs. Section 18746.2, any
legislative or administrative action, or any discretionary act involving
5 The one-year period commences when an employee is no longer authorized to perform the
duties of his or her state job, even if the person continues to receive compensation for accrued leave credits.
(Reg. 1 8746. 1 (bX 1 ); Coler Advice Letter, I-07-089.)
the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grarfi or corrtract, or the sale or purchase ofgoods or property.
"(A) Services performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the
requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract, or sale
agreement may be excluded from the prohibitions of this regulation,
provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings.
However, the prohibitions of Government code Sections g7401 and
87402 [the permanent ban on "switching sides,,] may apply.
"(6) The appearance or communication is made before any officer or
employee of any of the following:
"(A) Any state administrative agency that the public official worked
for or represented during the 12 months before leaving state office or
employrnent. . ..
"(B) Any state adminiskative agency which budget, personnel, and
other operations are subject to the direction and control of u.ry agency
described in subdivision (b)(6)(A). . . .
"(C) Any state administrative agency subject to the direction and
control of the Governor, if the official was a designated employee of the
Governor's office during the 72 months before leaving state office or
employment." (Reg. lS7 46.1 (b).)
B. Agencies Covered by the One-year Ban.
A designated employee's state administrative agency means the agency for which
he or she worked, or any board or commission under the agency,s control. (dould
Advice Letter, No. A-96-077.) The agency you work for is cai Fire. As a rlsult, you
may not for one year, for compensation, act as representative or agent for any person
before Cal Fire "for the purpose of influencing administrative or ligislatir" u"iorr, o.
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance, um"rd*"rrt, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, glrffit, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property." (Section 87406.) You may, however, appear before state adminiitrative
agencies other than Cal Fire, provided there is no prohibited conduct before an officer or
employee of Cal Fire involved in that appearance.
C. Communications Covered by the One-year Ban.
Communications restricted by the one-year ban include any formal or informal
appearance or oral or written communication made to influence legislative or
administrative action or any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment,
In summary, the one-year ban does not prevent you from requesting information
generally available to the public about agency business from Cal Fire or otler state
agencies. Nor does it prohibit you from attending informational meetings of Cal Fire or
another state agency regarding existing laws, regulations, or policies, so-long as, when at
the meeting, you do not attempt to influence the agency's legislative or admlnistrative
action. Social conversations with employees of Cal Fire and other state agencies, not
intended to influence administrative or legislative action, also are not prohibited by the
ban. (Tobias Advice Letter, No. 4-96-089; see section g7406(dx1).)
If you have arty other questions regarding this matter,please contact me at
(916) 322-s660.
